module-2-warehouse-stock-ledger-schema.label: Module 2 Project: Warehouse Stock Ledger Schema
module-2-warehouse-stock-ledger-schema.summary: Create warehouses and stock_movements through six cumulative schema steps.

ci_warehouse_schema_step1.title: Create the Warehouse Table
ci_warehouse_schema_step1.prompt: A regional warehouse team is replacing a spreadsheet with a stock ledger. In `schema.sql`, define `warehouses` with `id INTEGER` and `name TEXT`. In `query.sql`, verify the table exists by selecting `name` from `sqlite_master` where `type = 'table'` and `name = 'warehouses'`.
ci_warehouse_schema_step1.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.
help.hint_2: Click Run query and compare the results table with the expected result.

ci_warehouse_schema_step1.starterCode:
-- Provided verification: confirm that warehouses exists. Leave this query unchanged.
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='warehouses';
ci_warehouse_schema_step1.solutionCode:
-- Verify the table creation
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='warehouses';

query_sql.content:
-- Provided verification: confirm that warehouses exists. Leave this query unchanged.

-- Verify the table creation
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='warehouses';

ci_warehouse_schema_step2.title: Add Identity and Name Rules
ci_warehouse_schema_step2.prompt: Warehouse names must now be reliable business identifiers. Update `schema.sql` so `warehouses.id` is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` and `warehouses.name` is `TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE`. In `query.sql`, select the table's `sql` definition from `sqlite_master` to verify all three constraints.
ci_warehouse_schema_step2.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

ci_warehouse_schema_step3.title: Add an Operational Default
ci_warehouse_schema_step3.prompt: New warehouse locations should open as active unless operations says otherwise. Add `status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active'` to `warehouses` in `schema.sql`. In `query.sql`, return the stored table definition from `sqlite_master` so the default can be reviewed.
ci_warehouse_schema_step3.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

ci_warehouse_schema_step4.title: Create the Movement Table
ci_warehouse_schema_step4.prompt: The ledger also needs a place to record every receipt and adjustment. In `schema.sql`, create `stock_movements` with `id INTEGER`, `warehouse_id INTEGER`, `sku TEXT`, `quantity_change REAL`, `movement_type TEXT`, and optional `note TEXT`. In `query.sql`, verify that the table name appears in `sqlite_master`.
ci_warehouse_schema_step4.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the previous working code first, then make only the focused change for this step.

ci_warehouse_schema_step4.starterCode:
-- Provided verification: confirm that stock_movements exists after editing schema.sql.
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';
ci_warehouse_schema_step4.solutionCode:
-- Verify the table creation
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Step 4: keep warehouses and add the stock_movements table below it.
CREATE TABLE warehouses (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active'
);

-- Add stock_movements with id, warehouse_id, sku, quantity_change, movement_type, and note.

query_sql.content:
-- Provided verification: confirm that stock_movements exists after editing schema.sql.
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Define the stock_movements table
CREATE TABLE stock_movements (
    id INTEGER,
    warehouse_id INTEGER,
    sku TEXT,
    quantity_change REAL,
    movement_type TEXT,
    note TEXT
);

query_sql.content:
-- Verify the table creation
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

ci_warehouse_schema_step5.title: Protect Required Movement Data
ci_warehouse_schema_step5.prompt: Operations cannot accept incomplete movement records. Update `stock_movements` so `id` is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`; `warehouse_id`, `sku`, `quantity_change`, and `movement_type` are `NOT NULL`; and `note TEXT` remains optional. In `query.sql`, select the stored table definition to verify the rules.
ci_warehouse_schema_step5.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

ci_warehouse_schema_step5.starterCode:
-- Provided verification: inspect the stock_movements constraints after editing schema.sql.
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';
ci_warehouse_schema_step5.solutionCode:
-- Verify the table constraints
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Step 5: strengthen stock_movements with a primary key and required columns.
CREATE TABLE stock_movements (
    id INTEGER,
    warehouse_id INTEGER,
    sku TEXT,
    quantity_change REAL,
    movement_type TEXT,
    note TEXT
);

query_sql.content:
-- Provided verification: inspect the stock_movements constraints after editing schema.sql.
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Update the stock_movements table with constraints
CREATE TABLE stock_movements (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    warehouse_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    sku TEXT NOT NULL,
    quantity_change REAL NOT NULL,
    movement_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    note TEXT
);

query_sql.content:
-- Verify the table constraints
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

ci_warehouse_schema_step6.title: Connect Movements to Warehouses
ci_warehouse_schema_step6.prompt: Finish the warehouse ledger by connecting each movement to a real location. In `schema.sql`, give `movement_type` the default `'receipt'` and add `FOREIGN KEY (warehouse_id) REFERENCES warehouses(id)`. In `query.sql`, return the `stock_movements` definition from `sqlite_master` for the final review.
ci_warehouse_schema_step6.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the previous working code first, then make only the focused change for this step.

ci_warehouse_schema_step6.starterCode:
-- Provided verification: inspect the final stock_movements definition after editing schema.sql.
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';
ci_warehouse_schema_step6.solutionCode:
-- Verify the foreign key constraint
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Step 6: add the receipt default and link warehouse_id to warehouses.id.
CREATE TABLE stock_movements (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    warehouse_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    sku TEXT NOT NULL,
    quantity_change REAL NOT NULL,
    movement_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    note TEXT
);

query_sql.content:
-- Provided verification: inspect the final stock_movements definition after editing schema.sql.
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

schema_sql.content:
-- Update the stock_movements table with a foreign key
CREATE TABLE stock_movements (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    warehouse_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    sku TEXT NOT NULL,
    quantity_change REAL NOT NULL,
    movement_type TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'receipt',
    note TEXT,
    FOREIGN KEY (warehouse_id) REFERENCES warehouses(id)
);

query_sql.content:
-- Verify the foreign key constraint
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' AND name='stock_movements';

sketch-warehouse-schema-intro.title: Understanding the Warehouse Schema
sketch-warehouse-schema-intro.bodyMarkdown:
A regional warehouse team needs a small stock-ledger database before it can replace its shared spreadsheet. You are the junior database builder responsible for a two-table design that makes locations reliable and movement records traceable.

In `schema.sql`, you will build `warehouses` and `stock_movements`, then strengthen them with primary keys, required values, uniqueness, defaults, and a foreign-key relationship. In `query.sql`, each step verifies the stored definition through `sqlite_master`.

Your deliverable is a cumulative two-file workspace in which every stock movement belongs to a warehouse and incomplete operational records are rejected by the schema.
